{"body":"### Code of Conduct

- [ ] I agree to follow this project's Code of Conduct

### What would you like to see added?

The shell integration for `fish` offers the function to [repaint the current prompt](https://ohmyposh.dev/docs/faq#fish-display-current-bind-vim-mode). It would be nice if this was available for other shells too in order to allow for more scripting capabilities.","comments":[{"author":"JanDeDobbeleer","body":"@Ceedrich other shells have this functionality out of the box no?"},{"author":"Ceedrich","body":"I looked into `zsh` (since it's what I'm using) and it offers the widget `reset-prompt`. So, with a minimal pure zsh config (run with `zsh -df` then `source` the config):
```sh
# zshrc
PROMPT="%*"
bindkey '^B' reset-prompt
```

pressing `C-B` will successfully reload the default zsh prompt.
However, using a minimal `oh-my-posh` config:
```sh
# zshrc
eval $(oh-my-posh init zsh --config minimal.toml)
bindkey '^B' reset-prompt
```

```
# minimal.toml
"$schema" = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/main/themes/schema.json"

version = 4
[[blocks]]
alignment = "left"
type = "prompt"

[[blocks.segments]]
type = "time"
style = "plain"
[blocks.segments.options]
time_format = "15:04:05"
```

pressing `C-B` does not change the prompt. So yes, there is a builtin way to reset the prompt but it does not seem to work with `oh-my-posh`.

Update:
Consider the following zshrc. This works, i.e. pressing `C-B` resets the prompt.
```sh
autoload -Uz add-zsh-hook

set_prompt() {
  PS1="$(date +%H:%M:%S)"
}

update_prompt() {
  set_prompt
  zle reset-prompt
}

zle -N update_prompt

bindkey '^B' update_prompt

add-zsh-hook precmd set_prompt
```

I believe the `reset_prompt` widget does not trigger the `precmd` hook which is used by `oh-my-posh` to (re-)render the prompt."},{"author":"JanDeDobbeleer","body":"@Ceedrich alright, what's the use-case for this as it would require a bit of work to get this done."},{"author":"Ceedrich","body":"I would like to use it to make a vi mode indicator"},{"author":"ndrwstn","body":"@JanDeDobbeleer to offer another use, I have matugen re-theming elements based upon my wallpaper in hyprland, including my oh-my-posh prompt. 165	function _omp_serve_start() {
166	  _omp_serve_stop
167	
168	  # With job control active, an interactive zsh announces the coproc on the
169	  # terminal like any background job ("[1] 12345"). Disabling MONITOR for the
170	  # spawn suppresses the notice; as a side effect the daemon starts with
171	  # SIGINT/SIGQUIT ignored (POSIX no-job-control semantics), so Ctrl+C at the
172	  # prompt cannot take it down.
173	  setopt localoptions no_monitor
174	
175	  # The daemon's stderr must never reach the terminal (a Go panic would
176	  # corrupt the display).
177	  coproc "$_omp_executable" serve --shell=zsh 2>/dev/null
178	  [[ $? -ne 0 ]] && return 1
179	  _omp_serve_pid=$!
180	
181	  # Duplicate both directions to session fds: the duplicates survive a later
182	  # `coproc` (ours or the user's) replacing the coproc slot. The stderr
183	  # redirect (suppressing "no coprocess" when the daemon failed to start) must
184	  # be scoped to a block: on a redirection-only `exec`, zsh applies every
185	  # listed redirection to the shell permanently, which would silence the
186	  # session's stderr for good.
187	  { exec {_omp_serve_fd_out}<&p {_omp_serve_fd_in}>&p } 2>/dev/null
188	  if [[ $_omp_serve_fd_in -lt 0 || $_omp_serve_fd_out -lt 0 ]]; then
189	    _omp_serve_stop
190	    return 1
191	  fi
192	
193	  # Keep the daemon out of the job table: it must not show up in `jobs` or
194	  # inflate the job-count segment. Lifetime is governed by the fds - closing
195	  # them (or the shell dying) EOFs the daemon's stdin and it exits.
196	  disown %+ 2>/dev/null
197	
198	  # One session-long watcher delivers async records (segment updates and the
199	  # transient refresh) while zle is active. It never races the synchronous
200	  # read in _omp_serve_render: zle - and therefore this watcher - is not
201	  # active while precmd runs.
202	  zle -F $_omp_serve_fd_out _omp_serve_async_handler 2>/dev/null
203	
204	  return 0
205	}

281	    (( _omp_serve_failures++ ))

286	    # The daemon died since the last prompt - restart it once.

587	  # here-document swallows it as body text. A clean parse means the body was

589	  # can possibly be open.

608	  # editing starts. See https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/5992

611	  # this widget through it.
612	  local rawfunc=
613	  if (( $+functions[zvm_zle-line-init] )) && [[ $ZVM_INIT_DONE == true ]]; then
614	    zvm_zle-line-init
615	  fi

617	  # Start regular line editor.
618	  (( $+zle_bracketed_paste )) && print -r -n - $zle_bracketed_paste[1]

620	  local -i ret=0

624	    while true; do

628	      if ((ret)) || _omp_is_buffer_complete; then

630	      fi

634	      # here-document, whose terminator only matches at the very start of a line.
635	      BUFFER+=$'\n'
636	      CURSOR=$#BUFFER
637	    done
638	  else
639	    zle .recursive-edit
640	    ret=$?

643	  (( $+zle_bracketed_paste )) && print -r -n - $zle_bracketed_paste[2]

665	    # mirroring the empty-buffer workaround above), saves a CLI call

670	  fi

673	  if ((ret)); then

681	  fi

Base directory for this skill: D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\code-changes

# Code Changes

The workflow for going from an issue, pull request, idea, or feature request to shipped code.
Follow the phases in order. Analysis always comes first; code comes last.

Each phase has a reference file with the full instructions. **Read the reference file when you
enter the phase** — not before, and never skip it because the phase "looks obvious".

## Roles

Assign work based on model capability:

| Role         | Capability tier                               | Owns                                      |
| ------------ | --------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Orchestrator | Strongest reasoning model available           | Analysis, plan, supervision, verification |
| Implementer  | Mid-tier coder; small model for trivial edits | Executing one pinned, self-contained task |

Concrete model names per vendor (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) and how to run the split in Claude
Code, GitHub Copilot, Codex, or IDE agents: [references/model-tiers.md](references/model-tiers.md).

When the current agent already runs on an implementer-tier model, skip delegation: plan and
execute directly, still following every phase. Never delegate to a model more capable than the
current one.

## The flow

1. **Analyze** ([references/analyze.md](references/analyze.md)) — root cause and scope,
   validated against the code, never against the report alone.
2. **Plan** ([references/plan.md](references/plan.md)) — pinned spec, task split, parallel vs
   sequential, workspace per task.
3. **Delegate** ([references/delegate.md](references/delegate.md)) — match each task to the
   cheapest capable executor.
4. **Supervise** ([references/supervise.md](references/supervise.md)) — monitor, unblock, and
   critically review implementer output.
5. **Verify** ([references/verify.md](references/verify.md)) — quality gates plus functional
   proof, never delegated.
6. **Deliver** ([references/deliver.md](references/deliver.md)) — conventional commits and an
   outcome-first report.

**Stop gate:** Phase 1 ends with reporting the analysis and proposed approach to the user and
waiting for a go. Skip the gate only when the user already gave the go in the request itself
("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with Sonnet").

## Special cases

These entry points replace or extend Phase 1; the rest of the flow applies unchanged.

- Pull request review comments →
  [references/pr-review-comments.md](references/pr-review-comments.md)
- Issue triage → [references/issue-triage.md](references/issue-triage.md)

Analysis complete. Here's the report.

## Analysis: [#7427 — repaint_prompt function for other shells](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/7427)

### What's being asked

Fish's integration exposes a public `omp_repaint_prompt` function ([omp.fish:746](src/shell/scripts/omp.fish:746)) that invalidates the cached prompt (`_omp_new_prompt=1`) and triggers a repaint. It's documented in the FAQ ([faq.mdx:286](website/docs/faq.mdx:286), [faq.mdx:320](website/docs/faq.mdx:320)) for vi-mode indicators and directory-history tracking. The request is to offer the same in other shells.

The core problem the requester hit in zsh: `zle reset-prompt` only redraws the existing `PS1` string — it does not re-run `precmd`, so oh-my-posh never re-renders. That diagnosis is correct: [omp.zsh:426](src/shell/scripts/omp.zsh:426) renders only from the `precmd` hook.

### What changed since the issue was filed (March 2026)

The requester's actual use case — a zsh vi-mode indicator — was solved separately in June 2026 by commit `11094db2`: the `vimode` segment plus `_omp_enable_vimode` ([omp.zsh:742](src/shell/scripts/omp.zsh:742)), which re-renders on keymap change. PowerShell has the equivalent via `Enable-PoshVIMode` ([omp.ps1:1178](src/shell/scripts/omp.ps1:1178)). So the remaining demand is the **general-purpose repaint hook** — e.g. the commenter re-theming the prompt with matugen on wallpaper change, and arbitrary user scripting (`bindkey`, signal traps).

No open PR references this issue.

### Proposed change

Add a public `omp_repaint_prompt` per shell where it's feasible, mirroring fish's contract:

- **zsh** (the primary ask, small): a function that re-renders synchronously and refreshes the display — exactly the existing `_omp_render_vimode` pattern ([omp.zsh:736](src/shell/scripts/omp.zsh:736)) minus the `POSH_VI_MODE` export: `eval "$(_omp_get_prompt primary --eval)"` followed by `zle reset-prompt 2>/dev/null` (safe both inside and outside zle). Also register it as a widget (`zle -N omp_repaint_prompt`) so `bindkey '^B' omp_repaint_prompt` works out of the box. Like the vi-mode handler, it takes the sync CLI path, deliberately bypassing the serve daemon/stream — acceptable at ~70 ms per invocation.
- **pwsh** (small): a public function doing what the `ViModeChangeHandler` already does at [omp.ps1:1204](src/shell/scripts/omp.ps1:1204) — set `$script:Streaming.State = 'NEW'`, wrap encoding in UTF-8, call `[Microsoft.PowerShell.PSConsoleReadLine]::InvokePrompt()`.
- **docs**: generalize the FAQ entries that currently present `omp_repaint_prompt` as fish-only.

### Intentionally out of scope

- **bash**: readline has no supported way to re-evaluate and repaint the current prompt programmatically; anything here would be a fragile hack.
- **nu, elvish, xonsh, yash**: their line editors either re-run the prompt closure themselves or offer no repaint primitive; no demand shown in the issue.
- **cmd/clink**: `clink.refilterprompt()` exists, so it's *possible*, but I'd leave it out unless you want parity there too.

### Open questions

1. **pwsh naming**: keep the cross-shell name `omp_repaint_prompt` (consistent docs, one name everywhere) or a Verb-Noun cmdlet like `Invoke-PoshPromptRepaint`? I'd lean cross-shell name for doc symmetry, despite the convention breach — but it's your call.
2. Scope confirmation: zsh + pwsh + docs, or zsh only?

**Stop gate:** waiting for your go before planning/implementing.

I want  Invoke-PoshPromptRepaint
Yes, scope confirmed

2	
3	The workflow talks about capability tiers, not vendor names. Map whatever stack is in use onto
4	these three tiers. The model names below are a mid-2026 snapshot — they go stale; when a name no
5	longer exists, map its replacement by tier, not by nostalgia.
6	
7	## The three tiers
8	
9	| Tier         | Role                | Anthropic         | OpenAI            | Google            |
10	| ------------ | ------------------- | ----------------- | ----------------- | ----------------- |
11	| Orchestrator | Analysis, oversight | Fable 5, Opus 4.8 | Sol, GPT-5 (high) | Gemini 2.5 Pro    |

15	- **Orchestrator** — the strongest reasoning model available. Pays for itself on root-cause
16	  analysis, architectural judgment, reviewing implementer diffs, and catching wrong-but-plausible
17	  fixes. Everything the workflow forbids delegating runs here.
18	- **Implementer** — the capable code monkey: fast, cheap, and reliable when handed a pinned spec
19	  with decisions already made. Falls over when asked to make judgment calls, which is exactly why
20	  the spec removes them.
21	- **Trivial** — small, very fast models for mechanical, unambiguous edits: renames, config
22	  tweaks, typo fixes, doc touch-ups. Batch several to amortize the dispatch overhead. When in
23	  doubt between trivial and implementer, pick implementer — a wrong cheap edit costs more than
24	  the price difference.

# Feature
GitHub issue #7427: fish's oh-my-posh integration exposes a public `omp_repaint_prompt` function that forces a prompt re-render on demand (see `src/shell/scripts/omp.fish` line ~746). Add the equivalent to zsh and PowerShell, and document it in the website FAQ. All decisions below are final — do not relitigate them.

# Required reading before editing
- `.claude/skills/powershell/SKILL.md` (PowerShell conventions) before touching omp.ps1
- `.claude/skills/markdown/SKILL.md` (markdown conventions) before touching faq.mdx
- Do NOT run Vale on the .mdx file (it is CI-only for agent docs in this repo).

# Change 1 — src/shell/scripts/omp.zsh
Add a public `omp_repaint_prompt` function at the end of the file, right before the `# legacy functions` comment block (which precedes `enable_poshtransientprompt`). It mirrors the existing `_omp_render_vimode` pattern (line ~736) minus the POSH_VI_MODE export:

```zsh
# This can be called by the user whenever re-rendering is required.
function omp_repaint_prompt() {
  eval "$(_omp_get_prompt primary --eval)"
  zle .reset-prompt 2>/dev/null
}

# Allow direct key binding: bindkey '^B' omp_repaint_prompt
zle -N omp_repaint_prompt
```

Notes pinned by the orchestrator:
- Use `zle .reset-prompt` (leading dot, bypasses user wrappers — consistent with `_omp_render_vimode` and `_omp_zle-line-init`).
- `2>/dev/null` makes it safe to call when zle is not active (e.g. from a signal trap between prompts).
- Match the file's existing 2-space indentation and function style.

# Change 2 — src/shell/scripts/omp.ps1
Add a public function `Invoke-PoshPromptRepaint` inside the `New-Module` scriptblock, placed directly after the `Enable-PoshVIMode` function (ends around line 1214). It re-renders the prompt on demand, mirroring what the ViModeChangeHandler body does (lines ~1202-1212):

```powershell
function Invoke-PoshPromptRepaint {
    if ($script:ConstrainedLanguageMode) {
        return
    }

    $previousOutputEncoding = [Console]::OutputEncoding
    try {
        $script:Streaming.State = 'NEW'
        [Console]::OutputEncoding = [Text.Encoding]::UTF8
        [Microsoft.PowerShell.PSConsoleReadLine]::InvokePrompt()
    }
    catch {
    }
    finally {
        [Console]::OutputEncoding = $previousOutputEncoding
    }
}
```

Also add `"Invoke-PoshPromptRepaint"` to the `Export-ModuleMember -Function @(...)` list at the end of the module scriptblock (line ~1298), after `"Set-TransientPrompt"`.

# Change 3 — website/docs/faq.mdx
Add ONE new FAQ section documenting on-demand repaint for all three shells. Place it directly before the existing `## Fish: Display current bind (Vim) mode` section (line ~283). Content requirements:
- Heading: `## Repaint the prompt on demand`
- Explain briefly: oh-my-posh renders the prompt once per command; when something changes mid-prompt (for example a theme reload or an environment variable set by a script), the prompt can be re-rendered on demand.
- Document per shell:
  - fish: `omp_repaint_prompt`
  - zsh: `omp_repaint_prompt` — mention it is also registered as a zle widget, so it can be bound to a key: `bindkey '^B' omp_repaint_prompt`
  - PowerShell: `Invoke-PoshPromptRepaint`
- Keep it concise (a short paragraph plus a small per-shell list or code fences). Follow the surrounding file's style. Do not modify the existing fish sections.

# Verification you must run and pass before reporting done
1. zsh syntax check via WSL: `wsl zsh -n /mnt/d/oh-my-posh/src/shell/scripts/omp.zsh` (adjust mount path if needed; if WSL or zsh is unavailable, report that instead of skipping silently).
2. PowerShell parse check: `pwsh -NoProfile -Command "[System.Management.Automation.Language.Parser]::ParseFile('D:\oh-my-posh\src\shell\scripts\omp.ps1', [ref]$null, [ref]$errs) > $null; $errs.Count"` — must be 0 errors (adapt quoting as needed).
3. Go embed still compiles: from `D:\oh-my-posh\src`, run `go build ./shell/...`.
4. If markdownlint is configured in the repo for website docs, run it on faq.mdx; otherwise skip and say so.

# Non-goals (must NOT change)

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\conventional-commit

# Conventional Commit

## Commit Message Structure

```text
<type>(<scope>): <description>

[optional body]

[optional footer(s)]
```

### Types

| Type       | When to use                                            |
| ---------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| `feat`     | A new feature                                          |
| `fix`      | A bug fix                                              |
| `docs`     | Documentation changes, no code                         |
| `style`    | Formatting, missing semicolons, etc. (no logic change) |
| `refactor` | Code change that is neither a fix nor a feature        |
| `perf`     | Performance improvement                                |
| `test`     | Adding or correcting tests                             |
| `ci`       | CI configuration changes                               |
| `chore`    | Maintenance tasks (updating deps, tooling, etc.)       |
| `revert`   | Reverts a previous commit                              |

Append `!` after the type/scope to signal a **breaking change**: `feat!:` or `feat(api)!:`
When a change breaks existing behavior, **both markers are mandatory**: the `!` suffix on the type
**and** the `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer. They always appear together — never one without the other.

### Scope

Optional. Use the name of the area affected, e.g., `segment`, `cache`, `config`, `ui`.
Omit when the change is truly cross-cutting.

### Description

- Required. One short imperative sentence, no period at the end. The full header line (type +
  scope + description) must be **72 characters or fewer**. Aim for **50 characters or fewer for
  the description itself** — this almost always keeps the full header within budget regardless
  of type and scope length.
- Use the imperative mood: "add", not "added" or "adds". Never past tense or present-third-person:
  ✗ `added`, `fixed`, `bumped`, `implemented` → ✓ `add`, `fix`, `bump`, `implement`.
- **Never mirror the input's phrasing.** If the request uses past-tense words (`updated`, `added`,
  `bumped`, `was removed`, `got regenerated`), convert them to imperative before writing the
  description: `update`, `add`, `bump`, `remove`, `regenerate`.

### Body

Optional. Add context about _why_ the change was made, not _what_. The diff shows that.
Wrap at 72 characters.

### Footer

Use for:

- `BREAKING CHANGE: <description>` (required when `!` is used; explains the break).
- Issue references: `Closes #123`, `Fixes #456`.
- Co-authors: `Co-Authored-By: Name <email>`.

## Workflow

1. Run `git status` to review changed files.
2. Run `git diff` and `git diff --cached` to inspect staged and unstaged changes.
3. Identify the **type** from the table above. Ask yourself: does this change **remove, rename, or
   alter existing behavior** that callers depend on? If yes → it is a breaking change: use `!`
   after the type/scope **and** add a `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer. Both markers are always required
   together.
4. Identify the **scope** from the files/area changed.
5. Write a short **description** in the imperative mood.
6. Add a **body** if the _why_ needs explanation.
7. Add a **footer** for breaking changes or issue references.
8. Stage the relevant files explicitly (avoid `git add -A`).
9. Commit with a message that preserves multi-line formatting when body/footer are present.

## Examples

```text
feat(segment): add Ramadan segment with Aladhan API
fix(cache): always store mod time
docs(readme): update installation instructions
refactor(config): simplify option parsing logic
chore(deps): bump github.com/shirou/gopsutil/v4
feat(segment)!: rename template property StartTime to Start

BREAKING CHANGE: template strings using .StartTime must be updated to .Start
```

## Validation Checklist

- [ ] Type is one of the allowed values in .commitlintrc.yml
- [ ] The commit message respects the rules defined in .commitlintrc.yml
- [ ] Scope (if present) reflects the actual area changed
- [ ] Description is imperative mood, no trailing period
- [ ] Full header line (type + scope + description) is 72 characters or fewer
- [ ] Both `!` after type/scope **and** `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer are present whenever the change breaks existing behavior
- [ ] No sensitive files staged (.env, credentials, etc.)

## What shipped

[#7427](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/7427) asked for fish's `omp_repaint_prompt` in other shells; per your confirmed scope this adds it to zsh and PowerShell, plus a FAQ entry. In zsh, [omp.zsh:747](src/shell/scripts/omp.zsh:747) defines `omp_repaint_prompt` (re-renders via the CLI, then `zle .reset-prompt`, mirroring the existing vi-mode handler) and registers it as a zle widget so `bindkey '^B' omp_repaint_prompt` works directly. In PowerShell, [omp.ps1:1216](src/shell/scripts/omp.ps1:1216) adds `Invoke-PoshPromptRepaint` (exported from the module; guarded for constrained language mode, mirrors the proven `ViModeChangeHandler` body). The new FAQ section documents all three shells ([faq.mdx:283](website/docs/faq.mdx:283)).

Implementation was delegated to a Sonnet 5 subagent per the pinned spec; I overrode one detail — the docs used `--` as punctuation, which appears nowhere else in the file, so I rewrote that sentence.

## Verification (run independently on the final state)

- Gates: `zsh -n` syntax check (WSL), PowerShell parser (0 errors), `go build ./shell/...` (embeds compile), markdownlint (0 errors).
- zsh functional proof in WSL with a `{{ .Env.TESTVAR }}` config: widget registered as `user:omp_repaint_prompt`; after changing `TESTVAR` from `one` to `two`, `omp_repaint_prompt` re-rendered PS1 from `VAL:one>` to `VAL:two>`.
- pwsh functional proof: `Invoke-PoshPromptRepaint` exported from `oh-my-posh-core`, the prompt re-renders on env change, and invoking the function raises no error.

## Loose ends

- The pwsh `InvokePrompt()` repaint wasn't exercised in a real interactive PSReadLine session (it's byte-for-byte the shipped vi-mode handler logic, so risk is low). Quick manual check if you want it: in a session with the new init, run `$env:POSH_VI_MODE='x'; Invoke-PoshPromptRepaint` with a template referencing it and watch the prompt update in place.
- bash/nu/cmd remain out of scope as agreed; if someone asks for cmd, `clink.refilterprompt()` makes it feasible.
